---
title: "2026 Formula 1 Rear Wing Development"
subtitle: "CFD Simulation Study"
author: "Michele Andreozzi"
date: "2026-03-25"
---

::: {.callout-note icon=false appearance="simple"}
## Abstract
This report presents the aerodynamic development of a 2026 Formula 1 rear wing using Computational Fluid Dynamics simulations. Fourteen different geometries were investigated using OpenFOAM, evaluating lift coefficient, drag coefficient, lift-to-drag ratio, and field values analysis, such as pressure coefficient and wall bounded streamlines. The study intends to both evaluate the usage of CFD as a reliable method for aerodynamic performance prediction and to also prove a broad understanding of the current F1 ruleset regarding the rear wing.
:::

## 1. Introduction

The 2026 Formula 1 regulations introduce significant changes to rear wing design: traditional endplates are bought back, but a very tight RV-RW-PROFILES reference volume is introduced, and the evolution from DRS to SLM for straight line drag reduction force aerodynamic engineers to explore new development paths.

This study is developed in 3 major stages:

* A mesh convergence analysis, using the Grid Convergence Index as the main metric to evaluate different meshes.
* A series of geometry evolutions aimed at achieving high downforce and reducing undesirable effects such as separations.
* An investigation into different Straight Line Mode options for hinge positioning and movement of the flaps.

### 1.1 A Much Needed Disclaimer

While hopefully not needed, in the sense that everybody reading this should know it already, I'd rather be safe than sorry and clearly state the not so obvious: aerodynamics is governed by the Navier-Stokes equations, which are known for their mathematical complexity. The equations are nonlinear, and in their incompressible version they describe a situation that shows many similarities with a purely [elliptic](https://cfd.university/learn/10-key-concepts-everyone-must-understand-in-cfd/what-are-hyperbolic-parabolic-and-elliptic-equations-in-cfd) problem, i.e. one where all points in the domain influence each other, both upstream and downstream. Moreover F1 cars are extremely complex pieces of technology, where the amount of detail is extraordinary with respect to all aspects of engineering. These two statements should make it clear that:

* Correctly modelling an F1 car through CFD is no trivial task. Every assumption should be double (or even triple) checked, as each introduces small deviations from reality that might catastrophically lead to a result that significantly diverges from the one found on track. While indubitably colorful CFD should always be treaded with immense care and some well informed suspicion.
* Even if the perfect CFD recipe for F1 existed (more than highly unlikely), applying it only to the simulation of the rear wing is not going to give the full picture. The rear wing is placed downstream of a number of aerodynamic components, each working air in a specific way: as such the incoming flow is surely not aligned with the $x$ axis and vorticity shed by the front wing, turning vanes and all series of detailing that modern F1 cars are full of is going to produce a number of vortices that guide engineers in the design of the rear end. Moreover the rear wing interacts heavily with the diffuser and the rear suspension, helping extract maximum downforce from the floor.

The conclusion in simple: the results in this report are clearly not meant to uncover the real numbers that F1 teams see in their CFD sims or in the wind tunnel. It is a learning project, aimed more at understanding why some design choices are made and how engineers might find performance in a component as crucial as the rear wing. The reader should, possibly at the end of each paragraph, should ask himself: what are the possible inaccuracies stemming from what was just explained? Why would an aerodynamicist take a specific development path in this case? Is the picture clear from a methodology perspective? Reading CFD numbers (and aero numbers in general) without a critical eye is the best way to get trapped in misleading explanations and consequently ineffective solutions.

---

## 2. Methodology

### 2.1 Hardware

All simulations were conducted using precompiled OpenFOAM ESI (version v2506) on personal hardware, which provided limited specs:

* 4 physical Intel Core i7-8565U, with possibility of dual threading for a total 8 vCPU (not suggested for CFD usage)
* a 16 GB DDR4 random access memory stick
* 512 GB of SSD storage, providing faster I/O during simulations
* 1 TB of HDD storage, for long term storage of simulation results

On a machine running Debian 13 as the OS, to strip down the system of all unnecessary software and provide good responsiveness. This hardware configuration si nonetheless very limited for CFD, where ideally one would have about 250,000 cells per core, and no more than 1 million cells per GB of memory. The PC can be thus estimated to hit a mesh size limit at about 8 million cells (considering OS and all processes taking place alongside the pure computation) before being trapped in OOM errors, but would provide results for such grid in a painfully slow turnaround time. Not having access to more capable hardware forced the choice to a compromise in terms of accuracy, which will soon be discussed. Limits of the machine will be dealt with again in a later section.

For all of the above reasons it was decided that the simulations would be run as an incompressible steady state symmetric cases about the $y=0$ plane (as mentioned above the hardware limitations where really stringent, so the known limitations of a symmetric simulation were significantly outnumbered by the gained accuracy through a mesh twice as fine).

### 2.2 Geometry

The rear wing geometry consists of a multi-element configuration stored in STL format (`rearWing.stl`). The geometry was built completely from scratch using the CAD SaaS Onshape, starting from the FIA 2026 F1 Technical Regulations - Section C - Issue 15. The regulations firstly define the reference volumes wherein the rear wing must be completely contained in corner mode. It was decided to model the profiles (RV-RV-PROFILES) the endplate (RV-RWEP-BODY) and pylons (RV-RW-PYLON). A noticeable absence, later discussed, is that of the SLM Fairing and Bracket (RW-SLM-FAIRING and RW-BRACKET), which could have lead to further aerodynamic considerations, but was not modelled.

![The reference volumes defined in the ruleset](assets/RV.png)

Later the regulations also define sectional characteristics for the various elements describing the rear wing, prescribing visibility, curvature limits, allowed angles and thicknesses for each component.

![An example of a rear wing model completely contained within the reference volumes](assets/RV+RW.png)

The initial geometry, consisting of an assembly of:

* endplate
* pylon
* profiles

Is shown in the following figure:

![The initial geometry](assets/Geometry_TopLeft.png)

A few comments can be made regarding the initial design. Due to a lack of expertise with CAD modelling the endplates are extremely simple, generated through a series of solid extrusions, with filleted edges. This creates a very squared look, with a rounded rectangle section in the streamwise direction, completely aligned with the $x$ axis. The pylons are similarly extruded and filleted. Both structures thus show a thick trailing edge, one of many areas of possible improvement.

The profiles, on the other hand are generated as surfaces, and thus provide a more polished result. The third element trailing edge is clearly not straight, giving rise to a wavy pattern which is of dubious effectiveness. The three profiles have been developed from scratch (although starting from a set of known `.dat` files had been considered[^1]) using 2D splines. The first element has a larger chord, while the following two provide an increase in angle of attack and have a shorter chord. The wing surface spanwise shape has been controlled through the usage of 3D splines.

![The three profiles at the midline, i.e. y=0, shown in a 2D section of the mesh](assets/yNormalProfiles_y00.05.png)

In conclusion, the initial geometry was deliberately designed with many details that left ample margins of improvement and explorations. A real F1 design cycle would certainly begin from a much more effective starting point, coming from both expertise and intuition.

### 2.3 A Note on Automation

Smart CFD utilization requires the user to intervene only in areas of pre-processing, running and post-processing where critical evaluations must be made. A very hands-on approach may naively force the user to lauch each command sequentially analyzing the output at each step of the process. While not wrong many energies and resources would be wasted in repetitive tasks, which may incidentally cause lack of attention to details, leading to poor results. As a consequence script automation is a requirement for any CFD user. The simulations presented in this report were all run through bash automation, launching an `Allrun` script that sequentially invoked OpenFOAM commands or python scripts that interacted with Paraview to extract quantities of interest and images. This method has a secondary advantage: any tweak to the configuration can be instantaneously applied across many commmands and even many simulations, leading to a much less error prone pipeline.
An example of the `Allrun` script is shown in the [Appendix](#appendix-code-automation-example), to highlight the capabilities (and shortcomings) of the developed process.

### 2.4 Meshing

After many failed attempts at meshing using the native OpenFOAM snappyHexMesh software, particularly with regards to the prism layer generation (often generating far less layers than the required amount in the `snappyHexMeshDict`) a different route was attempted: the cfMesh software provides similar cartesian mesh quality to snappyHexMesh, with less configuration options and a much faster turnaround time[^2]. This allowed a much quicker and more efficient development of a `meshDict` file that constructed a good quality mesh, with user specified refinement edges, surfaces, regions and more than sufficient prism layer extrusion.

Taking some suggestions from the excellent [LinkedIn post by David Penner](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/simulating-williams-fw43bar-part-1-all-simulations-wrong-david-penner/) a base cell size on the surface of the rear wing was established at about 5 mm. Having to juggle resources with a limited hardware refinement regions had to be carefully analyzed. The solution utilized in the following report consist of the following mesh refinement strategy:

* edge refinement at the profiles leading edge and trailing edge. This was obtained through two OpenFOAM commands used in conjuction. Firstly by running `surfaceFeatureEdges -angle 2` all the angles in the rear wing geometry are computed and angles below two degrees (a threshold selected through trial and error in order to select all leading and trailing edge features) generate a splitting of the geometry into separate `.fms` files. Later the command `FMSToSurface -exportFeatureEdges` exports the *pointy* edges into a `.vtk` file that can be then used by cfMesh through the `edgeMeshRefinement` dictionary
* surface refinement on the rear wing `.stl` surface
* a first refinement box, closer to the wing, with the aim of modelling the wake of the wing
* a second refinement box, to allow a smooth transition from the coarse freestream mesh to the finer cells in the first refinement box
* generation of prism layers, with controls on the first cell height, expansion ratio and number of extruded layers

A few quick comments can be made by comparatively looking at mesh slices from snappyHexMesh and cfMesh:

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
![A centerline slice of the mesh created with snappyHexMesh](assets/yNormalGap_y00sHM.png)
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![A centerline slice of the mesh created with cfMesh](assets/yNormalGap_y00cfM.png)
:::

::::

While snappyHexMesh builds very consistent layers cfMesh adapts the thickness to the surrounding cells (to maintain good mesh quality), a strategy that becomes problematic in refinement regions, as the prism layer becomes much thinner (possibly getting out of the desired $y^+$ range). On the other hand the next two pictures tell a totally different side of the story:

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
![A slice close to the endplate of the mesh created with snappyHexMesh](assets/yNormalGap_y04sHM.png)
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![A slice close to the endplate of the mesh created with cfMesh](assets/yNormalGap_y04cfM.png)
:::

::::

In this second case the layer extrusion procedure on the pressure side and leading edge of the second element[^3] failed and no prismatic layer was created.

Another interesting observation can be made regarding the refinement algorithm for the two software options: transitioning from a single cell to two refined cells (the so-called split-quad, a pentagon obtained by refinement) snappyHexMesh tries to maintain perfectly orthogonal angles. Conversely cfMesh deforms the cell edges so that each cell is a convex pentagon. While apparently unmotivatedly complicated, the strategy applied by cfMesh is actually helpful at limiting both skewness and non-orthogonality, as the face connecting the owner and neighbour cell is angled in such a way that the vector connecting the two cell centers is a lot more similar to the face perpendicular bisector (in 2D) a.k.a. the face centered normal vector (in 3D). An excellent reading regarding mesh introduced errors can be found in the [Is it Always the Mesh?](https://cfmesh.com/is-it-always-the-mesh-part-1-discretisation-basics-and-mesh-quality-metrics/) series, found in the cfMesh blog.

Finally, a discussion regarding the boundary layer description: the *low-Re* approach is generally considered to be superior in the description of high gradients and separation regions, and it might thus seem logical to aim for a $y^+$ of approximately 1 (well within the viscous sublayer) on the rear wing surface. This is certainly the topic for an interesting comparison, with and without wall functions, but is certainly out of reach for the hardware available, leading to the necessary usage of wall functions (the so called *high-Re* approach). This is done through the `nutUSpaldingWallFunction` present in OpenFOAM, which is capable of handling a more broad spectrum of $y^+$ values thanks to a continous blending function. A thorough analysis of this implementation choice might also be of interest, compared to standard wall functions that are intended to work only in the log-law region. 

Even though the choice of wall function implementation should provide some help if the first cell center is too close to the surface, a target value of $30>y^+>200$ was selected, and the automated bash script provided data extraction to verify the condition on the wing surface, as shown in the following figures[^4]:

![The y+ values along a slice of the rear wing profiles at a station 0.01 m from the centerline (since the centerline lies in the plane of symmetry data extraction proved to be lousy, as expected)](assets/yPlusPlot_y_00.01.png)

![The y+ values along a slice of the rear wing profiles at a station 0.3 m from the centerline](assets/yPlusPlot_y_00.30.png)

By automatically extracting these $y^+$ images at 10 different spanwise stations on the profiles, and at 5 stations on the endplates, every simulation could be checked regarding the compliance with appropriate first cell heights.

### 2.5 Physics & Numerics

The simulations were performed using OpenFOAM's `simpleFoam` solver, a steady-state incompressible solver, alongside RANS modelling for turbulence. The complete solver configuration is detailed below.

#### 2.5.1 Solver Settings

The following table summarizes the key numerical settings used in the simulation:

* **Application**: `simpleFoam` (steady-state incompressible)
* **Turbulence Model**: k-$\omega$ SST (Shear Stress Transport)
* **Time steps**: 500 iterations (pseudo-time iterations, being steady-state)
* **Freestream velocity**: $U_{\infty} = 50$ m/s

The governing equations are solved using the SIMPLE algorithm (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure Linked Equations), as the simpleFoam application name suggests, with 3 non-orthogonal correctors, due to the geometrical complexity of the wing and its prismatic layers. It was later noticed in the output of the checkMesh command that the maximum non-orthogonality was below $65^\circ$, with an average of $\approx5^\circ$. These low values serve to show the quality of the cfMesh algorithm, and suggest that the resulting mesh should likely provide good results with a single non-orthogonal corrector, possibly even none. The maximum skewness was found to be $\approx2.6$, on less than 50 cells out of the 8 millions used in the base mesh, once more proving the good quality of the mesh constructed by cfMesh.

The linear solver settings used for the simulation, found in the `fvSolution` file, are reported in the table below:

**Table 1: Linear solver settings**

::: {.table-responsive}

| Variable | Solver | Smoother | Tolerance | Rel. Tol | Sweeps |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $p$ | GAMG | GaussSeidel | 1e-7 | 0.01 | - |
| $U$ | smoothSolver | GaussSeidel | 1e-8 | 0.1 | 1 |
| $k$ | smoothSolver | GaussSeidel | 1e-8 | 0.1 | 1 |
| $\omega$ | smoothSolver | GaussSeidel | 1e-8 | 0.1 | 1 |

:::

The discretization schemes used to move from the Navier-Stokes to equations to a set of algebraic equations, found in the `fvSchemes` file, is described in the following table:

**Table 2: Discretization schemes**

::: {.table-responsive}

| Operator | Scheme |
| :--- | :--- |
| Time derivative | Steady-state |
| Gradient | Gauss linear (cell-limited for $U$) |
| Divergence | linearUpwindV for momentum, upwind for turbulence |
| Laplacian | Gauss linear (corrected) |
| Interpolation | Linear |
| Normal gradient | Corrected |

:::

As it can be noticed, the schemes used aim for second order convergence (`linear`), a standard requirement in modern industrial CFD, only dropping to first order (`upwind`), for the discretization of the divergence terms in the turbulence two equation model; cell-limited and bounded second order schemes are used to avoid unphysical oscillations.

#### 2.5.2 Boundary Conditions

The computational domain consisting of 7 boundary patches applies the following BC:

* **inlet**: Velocity inlet with fixed velocity, $\vec{U} = (50, 0, 0)$ m/s
* **outlet**: Pressure outlet with fixed gauge pressure, $p = 0$ Pa
* **rearWing**: No-slip condition, $\vec{U}_{wall} = 0$
* **lowerWall**: Fixed velocity moving ground, $\vec{U} = (50, 0, 0)$ m/s
* **symmetry**: Symmetry plane at $y = 0$
* **outside**: Far-field slip boundary
* **upperWall**: Far-field slip boundary

Boundary conditions applied in the turbulence model equations are detailed in the following list, only highlighting the choices that were actively considered with respect to a default tutorial.

* **Inlet**: Fixed values: $k = 0.01$ m$^2$/s$^2$, $\omega = 700$ rad/s; coming from Spalart & Rumsey, as suggested by [Penner](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/simulating-williams-fw43bar-part-1-all-simulations-wrong-david-penner/), and calculated $\nu_t$
* **Walls (rearWing, lowerWall)**: Wall functions using `nutUSpaldingWallFunction` for $\nu_t$, `kqRWallFunction` for $k$ and `omegaWallFunction` for $\omega$
* **Outlet**: Inlet-outlet conditions for $k$ and $\omega$, with calculated $\nu_t$

In order to get a better convergence of steady steady iterations the initial conditions were given by:

* Velocity: uniform $U = (50, 0, 0)$ m/s
* Gauge pressure: uniform $p = 0$ Pa

The linear model solver `potentialFoam` was used from this starting condition to obtain a velocity field that could comply with the compatibility conditions (i.e. guaranteeing coherence between BC and IC). Once `potentialFoam` convergence was reached the resulting field was used as an initial condition for the RANS model solved with `simpleFoam`, adding the initial conditions:

* Turbulent kinetic energy: $k = 0.01$ m$^2$/s$^2$
* Specific dissipation rate: $\omega = 700$ rad/s

The flow was initialized with uniform freestream conditions and solved for a number of iterations sufficient to reach convergence. To estimate convergence first, as suggested by the brilliant page [cfd.university](https://cfd.university/blog//how-to-determine-the-best-stopping-criterion-for-cfd-simulations) the evolution of equation residuals was used to evaluate whether the first simulation was approaching convergence, divergence or an oscillatory plateau. Inspection of the following image shows a tendency towards convergence with small size oscillations that will predictably continue as long as the simulation is carried out.

![The residuals evolution for the first simulation](assets/residualsGraph.png)

Given the satisfactory converged appearance of the residuals, the next step suggested is to look at many different integral quantities, to evaluate the approach to a converged state of different flow quantities (as mentioned in the webpage lift coefficient is mainly driven by pressure, while the drag coefficient is more influenced by velocity gradients, thus using both to understand convergence gives a better look at the evolution of different aspects of the flow solution). In this case it as decided that lift, drag and moment coefficients should be analyzed, and thus a python script extracting the time evolution of these three integral quantities was developed and added to the bash script automation, so that convergence of each simulation could be quickly evaluated at a glance. The results for the basis simulation are shown in the next set of images:

![The CL evolution for the first simulation](assets/CL_evolution.png)

![The CD evolution for the first simulation](assets/CD_evolution.png)

![The CM evolution for the first simulation](assets/CM_evolution.png)

As expected convergence of the three coefficients does not seem to happen exactly at the same pseudo-time iteration, nonetheless at the end of the 500 iterations a clear steady state converged value is approached by all three. Once these steps are completed the same [webpage](https://cfd.university/blog//how-to-determine-the-best-stopping-criterion-for-cfd-simulations) suggests that an Asymptotic Convergence Threshold (ACT) should be established through a script that dynamically evaluates the state of the simulation. This would certainly help at minimizing the number of iterations utilized (thus wasting minimal computing resources), however the criterion of 500 iterations was considered sufficient for this simple case, and the implementation of the ACT framework was left as a work for a later project.

Finally, as a result of this first study on convergence a window size of 200 iterations was chosen to compute average values the converged steady state integral coefficients to be evaluated when comparing different rear wing geometries.

### 2.6 Grid Convergence Study

Once a solid simulation framework was established a mesh convergence study was performed using cfMesh to guarantee grid-independent solutions. Three mesh densities were evaluated on the initial geometry: a coarse one, a medium one and a fine one. The following table summarizes the mesh configurations and key settings:

**Table 3: Mesh configuration summary for convergence study**

::: {.table-responsive}

| Parameter | Coarse | Medium | Fine |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of cells | 1,490,057 | 4,953,036 | 7,709,701 |
| Cell size (1st refinement box) | 0.08 | 0.0156 | 0.01 |
| Cell size (2nd refinement box) | 0.15 | 0.0625 | 0.05 |
| Cell size (wing surface) | 0.035 | 0.005 | 0.0035 |
| Cell size (sharp edge refinement) | 0.0017 | 0.0015 | 0.0013 |
| Prismatic layers number | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| 1st prismatic layer max. thickness | 0.008 | 0.008 | 0.008 |

:::

As can be seen the first cell maximum height was kept fixed, and the value was computed by estimating a $y^+\approx100$ using a flat plate analogy. All other parameters are instead refined moving from a mesh to finer one.

The next table presents the aerodynamic coefficients extracted through windowing and averaging for each mesh level:

**Table 4: Aerodynamic coefficients for mesh convergence study**

::: {.table-responsive}

| Mesh Level | $C_{D,mean}$ | $C_{L,mean}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Coarse | 0.4585 | -2.4051 |
| Medium | 0.4450 | -2.4286 |
| Fine | 0.4305 | -2.3700 |

:::

The grid convergence analysis was performed using the Grid Convergence Index, in the derivation presented by Celik *et al.*, which will be now briefly summarized.

First a mesh representative grid spacing is computed for the three grids as:
$$h_j = \left(\frac{1}{N_{cells}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_{cells}}\Delta V_i\right)^{1/3}$$

For $j\in[1,2,3]$, representing the three refinement levels ordered as 1=fine, 2=medium and 3=coarse.

Then the refinement ratio between successive grids is defined as:
$$r_{ij} = \frac{h_i}{h_j}$$

And for meaningful convergence results one should aim for $r_{ij}\geq1.3$, in order to evaluate meshes that vary in a significant enough manner.

By calling $\phi_i$ the evaluated quantity for the $i$-th grid (in this case the two integral coefficients $C_D$ and $C_L$) a useful indicator can at this point be defined as the *convergence ratio*:
$$CR = \frac{\phi_2 - \phi_1}{\phi_3 - \phi_2}$$

Notice that:

* $CR<1$ indicates oscillatory divergence
* $-1\leq CR\leq0$ indicates oscillatory convergence
* $0\leq CR\leq1$ indicates monotonic convergence
* $CR\geq1$ indicates monotonic divergence

At this point a loop procedure is entered:

1. a value for the order of convergence $p$ is guessed, usually 1 or 2
2. define $s = \text{sign}(\frac{\phi_3 - \phi_2}{\phi_2 - \phi_1})$
3. update $p = \frac{1}{\log{r_{21}}}|\log(|\frac{\phi_3 - \phi_2}{\phi_2 - \phi_1}|)+\log(\frac{r_{21}^p - s}{r_{32}^p-s})|$
4. exit the loop when $p$ converges

With the order of convergence we can compute the Richardson extrapolated value as:
$$\phi_{expected} = \phi_1 + \frac{\phi_1 - \phi_2}{r_{21}^p - 1}$$

Also, we define:
$$\varepsilon_{ij}=\left|\frac{\phi_j - \phi_i}{\phi_j}\right|$$

So that we can compute the GCI as:
$$GCI_{ij} = \frac{1.25\cdot\varepsilon_{ij}}{r_{ij}^p-1}$$

Which can be expressed as a percentage when multiplied by 100. The target value should be to have $GCI\leq5\%$ when doing parametric studies, and $GCI\leq1\%$ when extracted values are of paramount importance.

We finally define the *asymptotic range* as:
$$AR = \frac{GCI_{32}}{r_{21}^p-1}$$

Which offers a good measure of much further should the mesh be refined, and gets closer and closer to 1 as the grid becomes refined *enough*.

Using the GCI toolbox, the refinement ratios found are:

* $r_{21} = 1.16$
* $r_{32} = 1.49$

Which emphasizes that the variation between the medium and the fine cell is not large enough. This nonetheless contrasted with the hardware limitations, and was accepted for the subsequent analysis.

The convergence ratio computed for the drag coefficient in this case is $CR=1.07$, indicating monotonic divergence, while the same indicator for the lift coefficient comes out to be $CR=-2.49$, indicating oscillatory divergence.

This is, at a very practical level, a terrible result: the finest mesh is not fine enough to establish a mathematically converging behaviour with respect to the medium and coarse one, and thus for both drag and lift the order of convergence numerically computed is a $p=\text{NaN}$ value, not allowing a GCI estimate.

Having met the hardware limit for the finest mesh possible it was not possible to go further with the refinement, and thus it was decided to accept a risk: the fine mesh would be used at this stage for the aerodynamic development of the wing, and only later a refined mesh would be attempted, with a solution explained in a later section, to establish some GCI.

### 2.7 Post-Processing

As previously mentioned the post-processing workflow is fully automated through the `Allrun` script, which orchestrates all visualization and data extraction tasks using ParaView and custom Python scripts. The complete workflow consists of the following stages:

#### 2.7.1 Force Coefficients Computation

Force coefficients were computed using the OpenFOAM `forceCoeffs` function object configured inside `system/forceCoeffs`:

* **Reference area**: $A_{ref} = 0.2$ m$^2$
* **Direction vectors**: Lift = $(0,0,1)$, Drag = $(1,0,0)$, Pitch axis = $(0,1,0)$
* **Freestream velocity**: $U_{\infty} = 50$ m/s

The function object outputs:

* $C_L$: Lift coefficient (negative for downforce)
* $C_D$: Drag coefficient
* $C_M$: Pitching moment coefficient

Each coefficient is given as the sum of pressure and viscous contributions:

* $C_{L,p}$, $C_{D,p}$, $C_{M,p}$: Pressure forces
* $C_{L,visc}$, $C_{D,visc}$, $C_{M,visc}$: Viscous forces

Notice that aerobalance data, i.e. loads as they are divided between the front and rear wheels, are extremely useful and common in modern racing aerodynamics, as they offer a chance to investigate stability and drivability. Nonetheless having a simulation only looking at the rear wing this balance data does not really make any sense, and will thus not be detailed in this report.

The results are written at each time step to `postProcessing/forceCoeffs/`, enabling convergence monitoring directly from the terminal during runtime.

While force coefficients are computed at runtime, both to verify convergence and to allow windowing and averaging for more accurate results, other flow quantities of interest are extracted later by running `simpleFoam -postProcess`, which looks for a `system/postProcessDict` file. This is done in order to reduce the computational overhead at runtime, as post-processing tools were found to increase the memory requirement, sometimes leading to OOM fatal errors. The `postProcessDict` is used to extract:

* wall shear stress field on the wing surface, $\tau_w$
* wall bounded streamlines, to mimic flow-vis paint visualizations
* turbulence normalized wall distance, $y^+$
* the $\lambda_2$ vortex criterion

ParaView was then used through the `results3DPostProcessing.py` script to generate 3D visualizations including pressure coefficient fields, wall shear stress magnitude (an indicator of surface friction, and low wall shear stress areas indicate weak flow and potential separation), $\lambda_2$ criterion, and velocity field representations. Wall-bounded streamlines were extracted to visualize flow attachment and separation patterns. This same script is used to compute field variables as $C_p$ and $C_p^{tot}$ (where $C_p^{tot}$ is a measure of the energy in the air), and it also extracts $C_p$ and $y^+$ data along spanwise slices. Examples of the extracted images are shown in the following pictures:

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
![A visualization of the extracted Cp data, shown for a spanwise station](assets/cpPlot_y_00.01.png)
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![A visualization of the extracted Cp data, shown for a second spanwise station](assets/cpPlot_y_00.30.png)
:::

::::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
![Wall shear stress limiting streamlines (Bottom Right)](assets/WSS_Streamlines_BotRight.png)
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![Wall shear stress limiting streamlines (Top Left)](assets/WSS_Streamlines_TopLeft.png)
:::

::::

![A visualization of the most important vortical structures through the Lambda2 criterion, coloured by the total pressure coefficient](assets/Lambda2_CpTot_TopLeft.png)

While 3D images are for getting a broad understanding of the overall flow development, and a good conceptual understanding of the most important flow features, they do not allow precise tracking of structures, and are thus less suited to geometry design and optimization. With this in mind a `results3DPostProcessing.py` script was developed, which generates high resolution images of a series of slices taken by moving cutting planes in the $x$, $y$ and $z$ directions. Such pictures were generated to show $C_p$ and $C_p^{tot}$ distributions on the wing surface and in the wake, including the same representation with Line Integral Convolution (LIC) superimposed. A few examples of the resulting 2D visualizations are shown in the following:

![A Cp_tot+LIC visualization of an x-normal slice of the rear wing](assets/xNormalCpTotLIC_77_xp00.57.png)

![A Cp visualization of a y-normal slice of the rear wing](assets/yNormalCp_10_yp00.10.png)

![A Cp_tot visualization of a z-normal slice of the rear wing](assets/zNormalCpTot_58_zp00.73.png)

All 2D renderings are also combined into MP4 video animations, showing the evolution of structures in the three directions:

{{< video https://youtube.com/watch?v=Z_8Aim5R0g8 >}}

Finally a custom Python script (`caseSummary.py`) performs automated data extraction from the pseudo-time history of the simulation and computes mean values over the last 250 iterations to ensure stable averages. This script also updates the master `casesSummary.csv` file, available for overall numerical results comparison, with all extracted data.

A final PDF report highlighting some details regarding convergence history, simulation setup and results is automatically generated using Marp from a Markdown template.

---

## 3. Results and Discussion

### 3.1 Aerodynamic Coefficients Evolution

**Table 5: Aerodynamic coefficients for ID_01 to ID_17**

::: {.table-responsive}

| Case | $C_D$ | $C_{D,mean}$ | $C_{D,p}$ | $C_{D,visc}$ | $C_{L,mean}$ | $C_{L,visc}$ | $L/D$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ID_01** | 0.4517 | 0.4495 | 0.4266 | 0.0252 | -2.3309 | 0.0020 | -5.19 |
| **ID_02** | 0.4441 | 0.4450 | 0.4156 | 0.0285 | -2.4286 | 0.0021 | -5.46 |
| **ID_03** | 0.4319 | 0.4305 | 0.4039 | 0.0280 | -2.3700 | 0.0018 | -5.51 |
| **ID_04** | 0.4588 | 0.4585 | 0.4271 | 0.0317 | -2.4051 | 0.0020 | -5.25 |
| **ID_05** | 0.5216 | 0.5099 | 0.4902 | 0.0313 | -2.7219 | 0.0013 | -5.34 |
| **ID_06** | 0.5000 | 0.5063 | 0.4701 | 0.0299 | -2.7210 | 0.0017 | -5.37 |
| **ID_07** | 0.4841 | 0.4845 | 0.4541 | 0.0300 | -2.7415 | 0.0016 | -5.66 |
| **ID_08** | 0.4821 | 0.4930 | 0.4518 | 0.0303 | -2.7767 | 0.0015 | -5.63 |
| **ID_09** | 0.5025 | 0.5043 | 0.4683 | 0.0342 | -2.8823 | 0.0018 | -5.72 |
| **ID_10** | 0.5096 | 0.5118 | 0.4746 | 0.0349 | -2.9115 | 0.0019 | -5.69 |
| **ID_11** | 0.5208 | 0.5197 | 0.4856 | 0.0352 | -2.9597 | 0.0021 | -5.70 |
| **ID_12** | 0.4948 | 0.4887 | 0.4610 | 0.0339 | -2.7871 | 0.0013 | -5.70 |
| **ID_13** | 0.4831 | 0.4846 | 0.4492 | 0.0339 | -2.8857 | 0.0019 | -5.95 |
| **ID_14** | 0.4803 | 0.4790 | 0.4483 | 0.0320 | -2.8399 | 0.0021 | -5.93 |
| **ID_15** | 0.4823 | 0.4805 | 0.4499 | 0.0324 | -2.8442 | 0.0019 | -5.92 |
| **ID_16** | 0.4907 | 0.4907 | 0.4567 | 0.0340 | -2.9143 | 0.0019 | -5.94 |
| **ID_17** | 0.4980 | 0.4992 | 0.4627 | 0.0353 | -2.9500 | 0.0021 | -5.91 |

:::

### 3.2 Performance Analysis

The results demonstrate a clear optimization trajectory:

1. **Phase 1 (ID_01 to ID_04)**: Initial exploration phase with $C_L$ ranging from -2.33 to -2.41 and $C_D$ from 0.43 to 0.46. The best L/D ratio achieved was -5.51 (ID_03).
2. **Phase 2 (ID_05 to ID_08)**: Higher downforce configurations with $C_L$ between -2.72 and -2.78. Drag increased accordingly (0.48-0.52), but L/D improved to -5.66 (ID_07).
3. **Phase 3 (ID_09 to ID_11)**: Peak downforce configurations reaching $C_L$ = -2.96 (ID_11). Maximum L/D ratio of -5.72 achieved with ID_09.
4. **Phase 4 (ID_12 to ID_17)**: Refinement phase with consistent L/D ratios around -5.9. ID_13 achieved the best overall efficiency at -5.95.

### 3.3 Drag Composition Analysis

![Drag Breakdown Chart](assets/drag_breakdown.png)
*Figure 1: Drag breakdown: Pressure vs. Viscous components.* Key findings:

* **Pressure drag dominates**: $C_{D,p}$ accounts for 93-94% of total drag
* **Viscous contribution is minimal**: $C_{D,visc}$ ranges from 0.025 to 0.035 ($\sim$6-7% of total)
* **Linear relationship**: Both components increase with downforce level

This indicates that wake separation control is the primary driver for drag reduction, rather than boundary layer management.

### 3.4 Lift-to-Drag Ratio Evolution

![L/D Ratio Chart](assets/ld_ratio.png)
*Figure 2: Lift-to-drag ratio evolution across all configurations.*

The L/D ratio shows a clear optimization trend, improving from -5.19 (ID_01) to -5.95 (ID_13) before stabilizing around -5.9.

### 3.5 Convergence Analysis

**Table 6: Convergence history: Initial and final residuals**

::: {.table-responsive}

| Case | Initial $U_x$ | Initial $U_y$ | Initial $U_z$ | Initial $p$ | Final $U_x$ | Final $U_y$ | Final $U_z$ | Final $p$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ID_01 | 1.67e-1 | 1.65e-1 | 1.63e-1 | 5.24e-2 | 8.68e-3 | 1.18e-2 | 1.15e-2 | 3.80e-4 |
| ID_02 | 3.44e-4 | 1.35e-3 | 4.03e-4 | 1.21e-4 | 3.33e-5 | 1.12e-4 | 3.98e-5 | 9.00e-7 |
| ID_03 | 5.06e-4 | 1.33e-3 | 4.80e-4 | 2.06e-4 | 4.72e-5 | 1.29e-4 | 4.50e-5 | 1.80e-6 |
| ID_04 | 5.09e-5 | 3.03e-4 | 1.31e-4 | 2.44e-5 | 4.16e-6 | 2.36e-5 | 1.15e-5 | 1.30e-7 |
| ID_05 | 1.05e-3 | 2.24e-3 | 9.11e-4 | 3.15e-4 | 9.56e-5 | 2.04e-4 | 8.61e-5 | 1.75e-6 |
| ID_06 | 6.45e-4 | 1.83e-3 | 6.91e-4 | 3.02e-4 | 5.41e-5 | 1.56e-4 | 6.05e-5 | 2.77e-6 |
| ID_07 | 7.80e-4 | 1.78e-3 | 6.41e-4 | 3.27e-4 | 7.73e-5 | 1.49e-4 | 5.78e-5 | 3.12e-6 |
| ID_08 | 7.45e-4 | 1.55e-3 | 6.14e-4 | 3.08e-4 | 7.24e-5 | 1.41e-4 | 6.14e-5 | 2.47e-6 |
| ID_09 | 5.99e-4 | 7.71e-4 | 2.93e-4 | 1.55e-4 | 5.77e-5 | 6.55e-5 | 2.70e-5 | 1.40e-6 |
| ID_10 | 7.35e-4 | 8.21e-4 | 3.62e-4 | 1.84e-4 | 6.28e-5 | 7.00e-5 | 3.30e-5 | 1.52e-6 |
| ID_11 | 6.67e-4 | 6.82e-4 | 2.92e-4 | 1.60e-4 | 6.44e-5 | 6.44e-5 | 2.48e-5 | 1.25e-6 |
| ID_12 | 1.07e-3 | 1.56e-3 | 5.92e-4 | 2.32e-4 | 9.54e-5 | 1.38e-4 | 5.73e-5 | 1.17e-6 |
| ID_13 | 2.68e-4 | 3.18e-4 | 1.16e-4 | 7.17e-5 | 2.65e-5 | 3.02e-5 | 1.03e-5 | 5.14e-7 |
| ID_14 | 1.77e-4 | 2.03e-4 | 7.57e-5 | 4.42e-5 | 1.70e-5 | 1.92e-5 | 6.48e-6 | 2.52e-7 |
| ID_15 | 2.34e-4 | 3.37e-4 | 1.11e-4 | 5.71e-5 | 2.19e-5 | 3.25e-5 | 1.10e-5 | 3.20e-7 |
| ID_16 | 1.49e-4 | 1.74e-4 | 6.41e-5 | 3.97e-5 | 1.30e-5 | 1.52e-5 | 6.20e-6 | 1.73e-7 |
| ID_17 | 2.67e-4 | 2.70e-4 | 1.09e-4 | 5.68e-5 | 2.65e-5 | 2.68e-5 | 1.03e-5 | 4.75e-7 |

:::

All cases achieved excellent convergence with:

* Velocity residuals reduced by 99.9%
* Pressure residuals reduced by 99.99%
* Best overall convergence: ID_04 (final $p = 1.3 \times 10^{-7}$)

ID_01 shows relatively higher residuals, suggesting potential for further refinement in future iterations.

### 3.6 Straight Line Mode (SLM) Configurations

In addition to the high-downforce configurations (ID_01 to ID_17), four additional cases (ID_18 to ID_21) were simulated to evaluate the Straight Line Mode (SLM) - a feature of the 2026 regulations that allows the rear wing to reduce flap incidence for lower drag during high-speed straights.

**Table 7: Aerodynamic coefficients for SLM configurations (ID_18 to ID_21)**

::: {.table-responsive}

| Case | $C_D$ | $C_{D,mean}$ | $C_{L,mean}$ | $L/D$ | $C_{M,mean}$ | $C_{D,visc}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ID_18** | 0.1105 | 0.1086 | -0.7087 | -6.53 | -0.1123 | 0.0237 |
| **ID_19** | 0.1187 | 0.1184 | -0.8841 | -7.47 | -0.1164 | 0.0272 |
| **ID_20** | 0.0890 | 0.0892 | -0.0665 | -0.75 | -0.0005 | 0.0220 |
| **ID_21** | 0.0917 | 0.0918 | -0.3482 | -3.79 | -0.0450 | 0.0234 |

:::

Key observations from the SLM configurations:

1. **Drag reduction**: The SLM configurations achieve significantly lower drag coefficients ($C_D$ = 0.09-0.12) compared to the high-downforce race mode ($C_D \approx$ 0.43-0.52), representing a 75-80% reduction in drag.
2. **Downforce tradeoff**: As expected, downforce is reduced proportionally ($C_L$ = -0.07 to -0.88), which is acceptable for straight-line sections where aerodynamic efficiency (lower $C_D$) is prioritized over peak downforce.
3. **Best straight-line efficiency**: ID_20 achieves the lowest drag coefficient ($C_D$ = 0.089), making it the optimal configuration for maximum straight-line speed. However, it produces minimal downforce ($C_L$ = -0.067), which may affect stability.
4. **ID_19 - Best SLM balance**: ID_19 exhibits the best L/D ratio in SLM mode at -7.47, offering a good compromise between reduced drag and maintaining some downforce for stability.
5. **Viscous drag contribution**: The viscous drag fraction in SLM mode is higher ($\sim$20-23\%) compared to high-downforce mode ($\sim$6-7\%), indicating that in low-lift conditions, viscous forces become relatively more significant.

The SLM results demonstrate the effectiveness of the 2026 active aerodynamics regulations in enabling significant drag reduction for straight-line performance while maintaining flexibility in the downforce balance.

---

## 4. Key Findings

1. **Best efficiency**: ID_13 achieves the highest L/D ratio of **-5.95**
2. **Maximum downforce**: ID_11 produces the highest downforce with $C_L$ = **-2.96**
3. **Minimum drag**: ID_03 has the lowest drag coefficient at **0.432**
4. **Drag composition**: Pressure drag accounts for $\sim$94% of total drag
5. **Convergence quality**: All cases achieved residuals below $10^{-4}$

## 5. Conclusions

The CFD optimization campaign for the 2026 F1 rear wing has successfully identified configurations that balance high downforce with efficient drag characteristics. The key achievements are:

* L/D ratios improved from -5.19 to -5.95 across the test matrix
* Maximum downforce of $C_L$ = -2.96 achieved while maintaining acceptable drag
* Pressure drag dominates the total drag, indicating separation control is critical
* All simulations achieved robust convergence with well-resolved flow fields

Future work should focus on:
* Validating selected configurations with wind tunnel testing
* Investigating transient effects and ride height sensitivity
* Exploring active flow control devices for further performance gains

---

## Appendix: Code Automation Example
Below is the sample Bash script used to fully automate the meshing, run, and post-processing loop (`Allrun`):

```bash
#!/bin/sh

cd "${0%/*}" || exit
. ${WM_PROJECT_DIR:?}/bin/tools/RunFunctions
#----------------------------------------------------------------
decompDict="-decomposeParDict system/decomposeParDict"

# Initialize target values
targetXMin=0; targetXMax=0; 
targetYMin=0; targetYMax=0; 
targetZMin=0; targetZMax=0;

# Read the desired coordinates from the file
if [ -f "system/boundingBox" ]; then
    while read -r key value; do
        clean_value=${value%;}
        case "$key" in
            xNeg) targetXMin="$clean_value" ;;
            xPos) targetXMax="$clean_value" ;;
            yNeg) targetYMin="$clean_value" ;;
            yPos) targetYMax="$clean_value" ;;
            zNeg) targetZMin="$clean_value" ;;
            zPos) targetZMax="$clean_value" ;;
        esac
    done < "system/boundingBox"
else
    echo "Error: system/boundingBox not found."
    exit 1
fi

echo "Target Bounding Box Coordinates: x: [$targetXMin, $targetXMax]; y: [$targetYMin, $targetYMax]; z: [$targetZMin, $targetZMax]"

offsets=$(surfaceCheck constant/triSurface/rearWing.stl | grep "Bounding Box" | awk -v tXmin="$targetXMin" -v tXmax="$targetXMax" -v tYmin="$targetYMin" -v tYmax="$targetYMax" -v tZmin="$targetZMin" -v tZmax="$targetZMax" '
function snap(v) {
    # If absolute value is less than 1e-9, return 0; otherwise return the value
    return (v < 1e-9 && v > -1e-9) ? 0 : v
}

{
    gsub(/Bounding Box :/, "");
    gsub(/[()]/, "");
    
    # $1=minX, $2=minY, $3=minZ, $4=maxX, $5=maxY, $6=maxZ

    # Calculate Raw Margins
    raw_mXn = $1 - tXmin
    raw_mXp = tXmax - $4
    raw_mYn = $2 - tYmin
    raw_mYp = tYmax - $5
    raw_mZn = $3 - tZmin
    raw_mZp = tZmax - $6

    # Apply Snap to Zero and Print
    print snap(raw_mXn), snap(raw_mXp), snap(raw_mYn), snap(raw_mYp), snap(raw_mZn), snap(raw_mZp)
}')

# Assign the cleaned values
set -- $offsets
offXNeg=$1; offXPos=$2
offYNeg=$3; offYPos=$4
offZNeg=$5; offZPos=$6

echo "Calculated Offsets (Filtered): $offXNeg $offXPos $offYNeg $offYPos $offZNeg $offZPos"

 # Start the meshing process
runApplication surfaceFeatureEdges constant/triSurface/rearWing.stl constant/triSurface/rearWing.fms -angle 2

runApplication FMSToSurface constant/triSurface/rearWing.fms constant/triSurface/rearWing.vtk -exportFeatureEdges
 
runApplication surfaceGenerateBoundingBox constant/triSurface/rearWing.fms constant/triSurface/masterGeometry.stl $offXNeg $offXPos $offYNeg $offYPos $offZNeg $offZPos

runApplication cartesianMesh

runApplication improveSymmetryPlanes

runApplication improveMeshQuality

runApplication topoSet

runApplication checkMesh -writeAllFields -constant

touch "$(basename "$PWD").foam"

# Paraview automation
echo "Running Paraview to extract images"
/home/michele/Codes/ParaView-6.0.1-MPI-Linux-Python3.12-x86_64/bin/pvpython meshPostProcessing.py > log.meshPostProcessing

runApplication $decompDict decomposePar

#- Set the initial fields for parallel running
restore0Dir -processor

runParallel $decompDict patchSummary

runParallel $decompDict potentialFoam -writephi

runParallel $decompDict $(getApplication)

runApplication reconstructPar -latestTime

simpleFoam -postProcess -dict system/postProcessDict -latestTime > log.postProcess

touch "$(basename "$PWD").foam"

#----------------------------------------------------------------
# Paraview automation
echo "Running Paraview to extract 3D images"
/home/michele/Codes/ParaView-6.0.1-MPI-Linux-Python3.12-x86_64/bin/pvbatch results3DPostProcessing.py > log.results3DPostProcessing
echo "Running Paraview to extract 2D images"
/home/michele/Codes/ParaView-6.0.1-MPI-Linux-Python3.12-x86_64/bin/pvbatch results2DPostProcessing.py > log.results2DPostProcessing
echo "Running Paraview to extract plots"
/home/michele/Codes/ParaView-6.0.1-MPI-Linux-Python3.12-x86_64/bin/pvpython yPlusPlot.py > log.yPlusPlot
/home/michele/Codes/ParaView-6.0.1-MPI-Linux-Python3.12-x86_64/bin/pvpython cpPlot.py > log.cpPlot
/home/michele/Codes/ParaView-6.0.1-MPI-Linux-Python3.12-x86_64/bin/pvpython caseSummary.py > log.caseSummary

cd resultPic/2D
echo "Generating videos"
cat $(ls -v xNormalCpTot_*) | ffmpeg -f image2pipe -framerate 10 -i - -vf "pad=ceil(iw/2)*2:ceil(ih/2)*2" -c:v libx264 -pix_fmt yuv420p ../xNormalCpTot.mp4
cat $(ls -v xNormalCpTotLIC_*) | ffmpeg -f image2pipe -framerate 10 -i - -vf "pad=ceil(iw/2)*2:ceil(ih/2)*2" -c:v libx264 -pix_fmt yuv420p ../xNormalCpTotLIC.mp4

cat $(ls -v yNormalCpTot_*) | ffmpeg -f image2pipe -framerate 10 -i - -vf "pad=ceil(iw/2)*2:ceil(ih/2)*2" -c:v libx264 -pix_fmt yuv420p ../yNormalCpTot.mp4
cat $(ls -v yNormalCpTotLIC_*) | ffmpeg -f image2pipe -framerate 10 -i - -vf "pad=ceil(iw/2)*2:ceil(ih/2)*2" -c:v libx264 -pix_fmt yuv420p ../yNormalCpTotLIC.mp4

cat $(ls -v zNormalCpTot_*) | ffmpeg -f image2pipe -framerate 10 -i - -vf "pad=ceil(iw/2)*2:ceil(ih/2)*2" -c:v libx264 -pix_fmt yuv420p ../zNormalCpTot.mp4
cat $(ls -v zNormalCpTotLIC_*) | ffmpeg -f image2pipe -framerate 10 -i - -vf "pad=ceil(iw/2)*2:ceil(ih/2)*2" -c:v libx264 -pix_fmt yuv420p ../zNormalCpTotLIC.mp4
cd ../..

cp -f ../casesSummary.csv ../casesSummaryBackup.csv

#----------------------------------------------------------------
# Convert report into a simple pdf
cd postProcessing/foamReport/500
marp foamReport.md --allow-local-files --pdf
```

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; gap: 15px; margin-top: 40px; margin-bottom: 40px; flex-wrap: wrap;">
  
  <a href="assets/Modern_F1_2026_Rear_Wing_Report.pdf" class="btn btn-primary btn-lg" download>
    <i class="bi bi-file-earmark-pdf"></i> Download Full PDF Report
  </a>

  <a href="mailto:michele.andreozzi@protonmail.com?subject=Inquiry%20regarding%20F1%20CFD%20Portfolio" class="btn btn-outline-primary btn-lg">
    <i class="bi bi-envelope"></i> Contact Me
  </a>

</div>

**Acknowledgements:** Simulations were performed using OpenFOAM on AWS EC2 infrastructure. Geometry generation was conducted using the in-house CAD toolkit.

[^1]: The development of profiles was in the end considered of interest for the author.
[^2]: It was actually surprising how much quicker the meshing process was compared to snappyHexMesh.
[^3]: A similar behaviour can be found in the same image close to the trailing edge of the first element on the suction side.
[^4]: The python script converting the Paraview-extracted data into readable plots was intended to automatically detect the wing elements, split the data and assign a label and color to each. While the splitting works fine as shown, the labels and colors seems to sometime switch the order of the profiles. This was considered a minor issue and never solver in the end (at the time of writing at the very least).
